days.headline: First stage back after the rest day, Aurillac to Le Lioran — steep, irregular Cantal climbs that suit an ambush more than a set-piece mountain train.

picks.why: The dominant climber of the race so far and the rider most likely to seize on any hesitation from rivals.
Has stayed glued to the front of the GC group in the mountains and is a threat to counter-attack on steep, irregular gradients.
Built for exactly this kind of unpredictable medium-mountain terrain.

days.headline: A gentle, short run north along the Allier valley to Nevers — sprinters' day, with a fast, controlled race expected.

picks.why: Backed up his Bordeaux win with another sprint victory in Bergerac — the man to beat in a straight fight.
Runner-up in Bergerac and consistently the closest challenger when the sprint stays together.
Podiumed again in Bergerac and has the leadout support to contest a short, controlled sprint stage.

days.headline: A novelty start at Magny-Cours then east across open Burgundy farmland to Chalon-sur-Saône — another sprint day, but crosswinds could split the bunch.

picks.why: The in-form sprinter of the race with back-to-back stage wins; only a split bunch would seriously trouble him.
Third in Bergerac and building form; dangerous if the sprint comes down to a pure drag race.
Well-drilled leadout that holds up in a straight fight to the line and copes well if crosswinds cause splits.

days.headline: The longest stage of the race skirts the edge of the Jura to Belfort — distance plus constant rolling terrain makes this a strongman's breakaway day.

picks.why: A durable puncheur who can survive a long day out front in the escape.
Loves a long, rolling breakaway stage exactly like this one.
A classics powerhouse capable of forcing the winning move on a long, punchy stage.

days.headline: A Vosges classic into Le Markstein Fellering — short but relentlessly climb-dense, the kind of day where a GC team can set a trap.

picks.why: Leads the race comfortably and has shown no weakness on any climb so far — the rider most able to press an advantage.
The closest GC threat and a rider who has to attack on terrain like this to claw back time.
Quietly solid in the mountains so far and well placed to move up on relentless, punchy climbing.

fantasy.strategy: With the stage 8-14 transfer window open, hold your climbers through the rest day and lean on them again for the Le Lioran ambush and the Vosges trap at Le Markstein, since climbing days outscore flat ones in this format. Rotate in a pure sprinter — Merlier is the standout after back-to-back wins — for the short, controlled days into Nevers and Chalon-sur-Saône, and keep a durable puncheur like Godon or Turgis in reserve for the long Belfort haul, which offers no clean sprint finish. Save the double-points bonus stage for a summit finish later in the race rather than spending it in this mixed, transitional block.
